module.title: CLASSES & OOP II
module.subtitle: attr_accessor · state · methods · to_s
module.theory:
## Objects that REMEMBER

In sector 0x03 you built your first class. Now we go deeper: objects that carry **state** — data that lives *inside* the object and changes over time. A bank account remembers its balance. A stack remembers what you pushed onto it. The object is a little machine that holds memory between calls.

State lives in **instance variables** — names that start with `@`. Each object gets its own copy.

~~~ruby
class Drone
  def initialize
    @energy = 100   # this drone's private store
  end
end
~~~

Every `Drone.new` has its *own* `@energy`. Two drones never share it.

> INTEL — `@energy` is invisible from outside the object. You cannot write `drone.@energy`. The only way in or out is through **methods** you define. That is the whole point of OOP: the object guards its own data.

## Letting the world read and write: attr_*

Writing a getter method by hand is tedious:

~~~ruby
class Drone
  def energy        # getter
    @energy
  end
  def energy=(v)    # setter
    @energy = v
  end
end
~~~

Ruby generates these for you. Put one line near the top of the class:

- **`attr_reader :energy`** — makes a getter. You can `drone.energy` (read only).
- **`attr_writer :energy`** — makes a setter. You can `drone.energy = 50` (write only).
- **`attr_accessor :energy`** — makes BOTH. Read and write.

~~~ruby
class Drone
  attr_accessor :energy
  def initialize
    @energy = 100
  end
end

d = Drone.new
d.energy          # 100   (reader)
d.energy = 60     # sets @energy
d.energy          # 60
~~~

Note the symbol: `attr_accessor :energy` — a colon name, not a string, and NOT `@energy`. It names the *attribute*; Ruby wires it to the matching `@energy` behind the scenes.

> WARNING — A classic beginner mix-up: `attr_accessor :energy` vs `attr_accessor "energy"` vs `attr_accessor @energy`. Use the **symbol** `:energy`. Strings and `@`-names will not give you the accessor you want.

## Methods that CHANGE state

Most interesting methods don't just read — they *mutate*. They reach into `@vars`, change them, and the object remembers the new value for next time.

~~~ruby
class Counter
  attr_reader :count
  def initialize
    @count = 0
  end
  def tick
    @count += 1     # mutate the stored state
    @count          # return the new value
  end
end

c = Counter.new
c.tick   # 1
c.tick   # 2
c.count  # 2  — it stuck
~~~

The difference between **mutating** and **returning** trips up newcomers. `@count += 1` *changes* the object. The last line `@count` is what the method *hands back*. They are two separate jobs. A method can mutate and also return — but be deliberate about which value comes out.

> INTEL — The LAST expression in a method is its return value (no `return` keyword needed). If you want `tick` to return the new count, make `@count` the last line. If the last line were `puts @count`, the method would return `nil`, because `puts` returns `nil` — a very common surprise.

## Guards: refusing bad operations

A good stateful object protects its invariants. An account should refuse to go overdrawn. A stack should refuse to pop when empty. You enforce this with a **guard** — a check at the top of the method.

~~~ruby
class Account
  attr_reader :balance
  def initialize(start = 0)
    @balance = start
  end

  def deposit(n)
    @balance += n
    @balance
  end

  def withdraw(n)
    return false if n > @balance   # GUARD: refuse overdraft
    @balance -= n
    @balance
  end
end
~~~

Here `withdraw` returns `false` and changes nothing when the money isn't there. Sometimes you instead want to **raise an error** for a truly illegal move:

~~~ruby
def pop
  raise "stack empty" if @items.empty?   # explode loudly
  @items.pop
end
~~~

`raise "message"` stops the method and signals a failure the caller must handle. Tests can check for this with `assert_raises { ... }`.

> WARNING — `raise` aborts the method immediately. Put your guard FIRST, before you mutate anything, so a rejected call leaves the object untouched.

## A friendly face: to_s

When you `puts` an object or interpolate it into a string, Ruby calls its **`to_s`** method. The default is ugly (`#<Account:0x000...>`). Define your own to give the object a readable form:

~~~ruby
class Account
  def initialize(name, balance)
    @name = name
    @balance = balance
  end
  def to_s
    "#{@name}: #{@balance} credits"
  end
end

puts Account.new("Trinity", 500)   # => Trinity: 500 credits
"Balance — #{Account.new("Neo", 9)}"  # => "Balance — Neo: 9 credits"
~~~

`to_s` must **return** the string (last expression), not `puts` it. If you `puts` inside `to_s`, you print early and return `nil`, and the interpolation shows blank.

> INTEL — String interpolation `"#{obj}"` automatically calls `obj.to_s`. Define `to_s` once and your object reads nicely everywhere.

You now have the full kit: `@state`, `attr_*` accessors, mutating methods, guards, and `to_s`. Build objects that remember, defend themselves, and explain who they are.

exercises.title: THE VAULT
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Vault**.

- **initialize(credits)** stores the amount in `@credits`
- expose it with **attr_reader :credits** so the world can read `v.credits`

~~~ruby
v = Vault.new(500)
v.credits   # 500
~~~
exercises.hint: attr_reader :credits at the top, and store the argument: @credits = credits.
exercises.lore: The vault remembers every credit. It never forgets a debt.

exercises.title: CALL SIGN
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Operator**.

- **initialize(handle)** stores the handle in `@handle`
- use **attr_accessor :handle** so the handle can be both READ and CHANGED from outside

~~~ruby
o = Operator.new("Neo")
o.handle          # "Neo"
o.handle = "The One"
o.handle          # "The One"
~~~

tests.name: handle can be rewritten
tests.code:
o = Operator.new("Neo")
o.handle = "The One"
assert_equal(o.handle, "The One")

tests.name: accessor gives a writer
tests.code:
o = Operator.new("Trinity")
assert(o.respond_to?(:handle=), "need attr_accessor so handle is writable")

exercises.hint: attr_accessor :handle makes both a reader and a writer (handle=).
exercises.lore: Choose your call sign carefully. The grid will know you by it.

exercises.title: KILL TALLY
exercises.brief: A counter that mutates and returns its state.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Tally** that counts events.

- **initialize** sets `@count` to **0**
- expose it with **attr_reader :count**
- **bump** increases the count by 1 and RETURNS the new count

~~~ruby
t = Tally.new
t.bump    # 1
t.bump    # 2
t.count   # 2
~~~
exercises.starter:
class Tally
  attr_reader :count
  def initialize
    @count = 0
  end
  def bump
    # TODO: increase @count and return it
    @count
  end
end

tests.name: state sticks
tests.code:
t = Tally.new
t.bump
t.bump
t.bump
assert_equal(t.count, 3)

exercises.hint: Inside bump: @count += 1 mutates the state, then make @count the last line so it is returned.
exercises.lore: Every mark on the tally is a ghost in the machine.

exercises.title: PUSH DOWN
exercises.brief: A stack with push, pop and size.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Stack** backed by an array `@items`.

- **initialize** sets `@items` to an empty array `[]`
- **push(x)** adds x to the top and returns x
- **pop** removes and returns the top item (the LAST one added)
- **size** returns how many items are stored

~~~ruby
s = Stack.new
s.push("a")
s.push("b")
s.size   # 2
s.pop    # "b"
s.size   # 1
~~~
exercises.starter:
class Stack
  def initialize
    @items = []
  end
  def push(x)
    # TODO
  end
  def pop
    # TODO
  end
  def size
    0
  end
end

tests.name: push returns the item and grows
pop is last-in first-out
tests.code:
s = Stack.new
s.push("a")
s.push("b")
assert_equal(s.pop, "b")
assert_equal(s.size, 1)

tests.name: size tracks the count
tests.code:
s = Stack.new
s.push(1)
s.push(2)
s.push(3)
s.pop
assert_equal(s.size, 2)

exercises.hint: Array has .push, .pop and .size already — delegate to @items and return the right value.
exercises.lore: Last in, first out. The newest secret is the first to surface.

exercises.title: POWER METER
exercises.brief: Mutate a value but clamp it to a ceiling.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Meter** that charges toward a maximum.

- **initialize(max)** stores `@max` and sets `@level` to **0**
- expose the current level with **attr_reader :level**
- **charge(n)** adds n to the level but NEVER above `@max`, then returns the new level

~~~ruby
m = Meter.new(100)
m.charge(40)   # 40
m.charge(80)   # 100  (clamped at max)
m.level        # 100
~~~
exercises.hint: Use [@max, @level + n].min so the level can rise but never passes @max.
exercises.lore: The reactor will take only so much. Push harder and it simply holds.

exercises.title: OVERDRAFT GUARD
exercises.brief: An account that refuses to go negative.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Account** with a guard against overdraft.

- **initialize(start)** stores the opening balance in `@balance`
- expose it with **attr_reader :balance**
- **deposit(n)** adds n and returns the new balance
- **withdraw(n)** subtracts n and returns the new balance — BUT if n is greater than the balance it must change nothing and return **false**

~~~ruby
a = Account.new(100)
a.deposit(50)    # 150
a.withdraw(60)   # 90
a.withdraw(999)  # false  (refused, balance unchanged)
a.balance        # 90
~~~
exercises.hint: Put the guard FIRST: return false if n > @balance — before you touch @balance.
exercises.lore: The bank does not extend credit to ghosts. Insufficient funds, runner.

exercises.title: EMPTY BUFFER PANIC
exercises.brief: A queue that raises when popped while empty.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Buffer** (a first-in first-out queue) backed by `@items`.

- **initialize** sets `@items` to `[]`
- **add(x)** appends x and returns x
- **take** removes and returns the OLDEST item (the first one added) — but if the buffer is empty it must **raise** an error
- **empty?** returns true when there are no items

~~~ruby
b = Buffer.new
b.add("a")
b.add("b")
b.take     # "a"
b.take     # "b"
b.take     # raises — nothing left
~~~

tests.name: FIFO order
tests.code:
b = Buffer.new
b.add("a")
b.add("b")
assert_equal(b.take, "a")
assert_equal(b.take, "b")

tests.name: empty? reflects state
tests.code:
b = Buffer.new
assert_equal(b.empty?, true)
b.add(1)
assert_equal(b.empty?, false)

tests.name: raises again after draining
tests.code:
b = Buffer.new
b.add("x")
b.take
assert_raises { b.take }

exercises.hint: Guard first: raise "buffer empty" if @items.empty?  then @items.shift for FIFO.
exercises.lore: Reach into an empty buffer and the system screams. Nothing comes from nothing.

exercises.title: IDENTITY STRING
exercises.brief: Give an object a readable to_s.
exercises.prompt:
Define a class **Runner** with a custom **to_s** so the object reads nicely when printed or interpolated.

- **initialize(name, level)** stores both in `@name` and `@level`
- **to_s** RETURNS the string `"NAME [LV.LEVEL]"` — the name, a space, then the level in square brackets prefixed with `LV.`

~~~ruby
r = Runner.new("Neo", 7)
r.to_s              # "Neo [LV.7]"
"#{Runner.new("Trinity", 12)}"   # "Trinity [LV.12]"
~~~
exercises.hint: Build it with interpolation and make it the LAST expression: "#{@name} [LV.#{@level}]".
exercises.lore: On the grid you are a name and a number. Wear them well.
